title: C# Syntax
content:
### Basic Syntax Rules

C# syntax is similar to other C-family languages like Java and C++.

### Structure of a C# Program

```
using System;

class Program
{
    static void Main()
    {
        Console.WriteLine("Hello!");
    }
}
```

### Key Syntax Elements

| Element | Description | Example |
|---------|-------------|---------|
| Semicolons | End every statement | `int x = 5;` |
| Curly braces | Define code blocks | `{ ... }` |
| Comments | Document code | `// single line` |
| Case sensitivity | C# is case-sensitive | `Main` vs `main` |

### Identifiers

- Must start with a letter or underscore
- Can contain letters, digits, underscores
- Cannot be keywords (`class`, `int`, `void`, etc.)
- C# naming conventions: PascalCase for methods/classes, camelCase for variables

### Top-Level Statements (C# 9+)

Starting from C# 9, you can omit the class and Main method wrapper:

```csharp
// No namespace, class, or Main required
Console.WriteLine("Hello from top-level!");
```

> **Key Point:** Every statement in C# ends with a semicolon. Code blocks are enclosed in curly braces.

The `Main` method is the entry point of any C# application. It can return `void`, `int`, `Task`, or `Task<int>`.

quiz.question: What terminates every statement in C#?
What is the entry point of a C# application?
